summary: Machine policy for whether Windows diagnostic data may include the device name. Microsoft documents the same device policy path, default, and numeric meanings that the app writes.

setting.name: Device Name in Diagnostic Data
setting.casual_explanation: Windows diagnostic data can include the computer name. This policy decides whether that name is allowed to be sent with diagnostic data.
setting.why_people_change_it: Privacy-focused users and managed environments may want diagnostic uploads to avoid carrying a recognizable device name.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Turning this off reduces identifying information in diagnostics, but it can make troubleshooting and fleet analysis harder because uploaded data no longer carries the device name.
setting.professional_notes: DataCollection.admx maps this machine policy to HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\DataCollection\AllowDeviceNameInTelemetry with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Windows does not force the diagnostic-data device-name policy.

allowed_values.label: Do not include device name
allowed_values.meaning: The device name is blocked from diagnostic data by policy.

allowed_values.label: Include device name
allowed_values.meaning: The device name is explicitly allowed in diagnostic data by policy.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes AllowDeviceNameInTelemetry = 0, which matches the documented privacy-oriented policy state.

windows_defaults.label: Windows device-policy default
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows 10 version 1803 and newer supported systems

states.rationale: The machine policy is not configured by default.

windows_defaults.notes: This is the policy baseline and does not by itself describe the wider telemetry configuration of the device.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Devices that rely on standard Microsoft troubleshooting flows
recommended_profiles.good_when: You want Windows diagnostic behavior to stay at its normal policy baseline.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want to reduce identifying data in diagnostic uploads.
tradeoffs: Windows keeps ordinary control of this telemetry detail.
No hard privacy restriction is enforced for the device name field.

recommended_profiles.label: Privacy focused
intended_for: Managed devices with tighter diagnostic-data policies
avoid_for: Support workflows that benefit from directly identifying the source device

states.rationale: Block the device name from diagnostic data by policy.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want diagnostic uploads to carry less identifying device information.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You need easier device-level correlation during troubleshooting.
tradeoffs: Troubleshooting teams may lose a convenient device label in diagnostics.

evidence.title: Microsoft Policy CSP: AllowDeviceNameInDiagnosticData
evidence.summary: Official documentation for the device policy that controls whether the device name is included in diagnostic data.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft DataCollection.admx mapping
evidence.summary: The local ADMX file shows AllowDeviceNameInTelemetry with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0 under the DataCollection policy key.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes AllowDeviceNameInTelemetry = 0 for the current tweak.

evidence.title: Existing tweak provenance record
evidence.summary: The repo already tracks provenance for this tweak in the current catalog output.

decision.why: Microsoft documents AllowDeviceNameInDiagnosticData directly in the System Policy CSP, including the device scope, default value, allowed values, and the group policy mapping to Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\DataCollection. The app writes that exact machine policy surface with the documented privacy-focused value 0.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: AllowDeviceNameInDiagnosticData ... Default Value 0 ... Allowed values: 0 (Default) Disabled. 1 Allowed. Group policy mapping ... Registry Key Name Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\DataCollection
validation_proof.notes: The System Policy CSP page explicitly names the policy, gives the default and allowed values, and maps it to the same DataCollection machine policy key the app writes.
